section.title: The Promise of Restoration
section.sectionRationale: Still confined in the courtyard of the guard as the siege presses in, Jeremiah receives a second word of hope, an invitation to call on God and be shown great and unsearchable things. What follows is a promise of healing for the devastated city, and, at its center, the Righteous Branch of David who will execute justice and righteousness and bear the name The LORD Our Righteousness.
section.thesis: Even from a prison cell in a doomed city, God's covenant faithfulness is as fixed as the cycle of day and night, guaranteeing not only the physical rebuilding of Jerusalem but the coming of a righteous Davidic king in whom God's own righteousness becomes His people's name and hope.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: God's invitation to call and be shown unsearchable things; the promise of healing, joy, and forgiveness for the ruined city; the Righteous Branch of David and the name The LORD Our Righteousness; God's covenant with David and the Levites made as unbreakable as day and night; and the assurance that Israel's descendants will never be finally rejected.

units.label: Call to Me, and I will answer

verses.text: While Jeremiah was still confined in the courtyard of the guard, the word of the LORD came to him a second time:
“Thus says the LORD who made the earth, the LORD who formed it and established it, the LORD is His name:
Call to Me, and I will answer and show you great and unsearchable things you do not know.
For this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says about the houses of this city and the palaces of the kings of Judah that have been torn down for defense against the siege ramps and the sword:

The Chaldeans are coming to fight and to fill those places with the corpses of the men I will strike down in My anger and in My wrath. I have hidden My face from this city because of all its wickedness.

units.label: Healing and joy for the ruined city

verses.text: Nevertheless, I will bring to it health and healing, and I will heal its people and reveal to them the abundance of peace and truth.
I will restore Judah and Israel from captivity and will rebuild them as in former times.
And I will cleanse them from all the iniquity they have committed against Me, and will forgive all their sins of rebellion against Me.

So this city will bring Me renown, joy, praise, and glory before all the nations of the earth, who will hear of all the good I do for it. They will tremble in awe because of all the goodness and prosperity that I will provide for it.

This is what the LORD says: In this place you say is a wasteland without man or beast, in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem that are deserted, inhabited by neither man nor beast, there will be heard again

the sounds of joy and gladness, the voices of the bride and bridegroom, and the voices of those bringing thank offerings into the house of the LORD, saying: 
‘Give thanks to the LORD of Hosts, for the LORD is good; 
His loving devotion endures forever.’ 
For I will restore the land from captivity as in former times, says the LORD.
This is what the LORD of Hosts says: In this desolate place, without man or beast, and in all its cities, there will once more be pastures for shepherds to rest their flocks.

In the cities of the hill country, the foothills, and the Negev, in the land of Benjamin and the cities surrounding Jerusalem, and in the cities of Judah, the flocks will again pass under the hands of the one who counts them, says the LORD.

units.label: The Righteous Branch, The LORD Our Righteousness
units.note: a king and a priesthood secured forever

verses.text: Behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will fulfill the gracious promise that I have spoken to the house of Israel and the house of Judah.
In those days and at that time I will cause to sprout for David a righteous {{tsemach|Branch}}, and He will administer justice and righteousness in the land.
In those days Judah will be saved, and Jerusalem will dwell securely, and this is the name by which it will be called: {{yahwehTsidqenu|The LORD Our Righteousness}}.
For this is what the LORD says: David will never lack a man to sit on the throne of the house of Israel,
nor will the priests who are Levites ever fail to have a man before Me to offer burnt offerings, to burn grain offerings, and to present sacrifices.”

units.label: A covenant as fixed as day and night
units.note: as sure as the sun and stars

verses.text: And the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:
“This is what the LORD says: If you can break My {{beritYom|covenant}} with the day and My covenant with the night, so that day and night cease to occupy their appointed time,
then My covenant may also be broken with David My servant and with My ministers the Levites who are priests, so that David will not have a son to reign on his throne.
As the hosts of heaven cannot be counted and as the sand on the seashore cannot be measured, so too will I multiply the descendants of My servant David and the Levites who minister before Me.”
Moreover, the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:
“Have you not noticed what these people are saying: ‘The LORD has rejected the two families He had chosen’? So they despise My people and no longer regard them as a nation.
This is what the LORD says: If I have not established My covenant with the day and the night and the fixed order of heaven and earth,

then I would also reject the descendants of Jacob and of My servant David, so as not to take from his descendants rulers over the descendants of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. For I will restore them from captivity and will have compassion on them.”

tsemach.gloss: A branch or sprout, growth out of what seems dead. The righteous Tsemach that God causes to sprout for David is a Davidic king raised up in a moment when David's dynasty appeared finished; Zechariah later uses the same term of the same coming figure, Zechariah 3:8, 6:12.

yahwehTsidqenu.gloss: The LORD Our Righteousness, the name given to Jerusalem and, in Jeremiah 23:6, to the Righteous Branch Himself. It declares that the city's security and the people's standing before God rest entirely on a righteousness that is not their own but the LORD's, given to them.

beritYom.gloss: The covenant with the day and the night, God's fixed ordering of creation's rhythm. God stakes His covenant with David and the Levites on the same unbreakable reliability as the daily rising of the sun, an oath as certain as creation itself.

basic.context: Still imprisoned as Jerusalem's siege continues, Jeremiah receives a second word from God, an invitation to call on Him and be shown great and unsearchable things. The chapter promises healing and rebuilding for the devastated city, and at its center announces a righteous Branch of David who will bring true justice, a king under whom the city itself will be renamed The LORD Our Righteousness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real promise to a real, defeated nation, grounded in God's own unbreakable faithfulness rather than in Judah's merit, since the surrounding chapters have already shown how thoroughly Judah has broken covenant. The righteous Branch names a coming, specific individual, not merely a general improvement in national fortunes, as the New Testament's application to Christ confirms.
basic.originalLanguages: God promises to cause a tsemach, a righteous Branch, to sprout for David out of a dynasty that seemed finished, and under Him Jerusalem will be called by the name yahwehTsidqenu, the LORD Our Righteousness. This whole promise rests on the beritYom, God's covenant with the day and the night, as unbreakable as creation's own rhythm.
basic.typology: The righteous Branch who administers justice and righteousness, and under whom the city is renamed The LORD Our Righteousness, anticipates Christ, who is Himself made our righteousness before God, 1 Corinthians 1:30. The everlasting Davidic throne, secured by a covenant as sure as day and night, anticipates the eternal reign of Christ, the true and final Son of David, Luke 1:32-33.
basic.christ: Jesus is the righteous Branch this chapter promises, the Son of David who brings true justice and righteousness. Paul teaches that Christ Himself has become our righteousness, 1 Corinthians 1:30, fulfilling the very name given to the city here, The LORD Our Righteousness, so that believers, not just Jerusalem, now bear that righteousness as their own standing before God.
basic.god: God is the one who hides His face in righteous judgment yet promises health, healing, and full forgiveness on the other side of that judgment. His covenant faithfulness to David and to His people is grounded not in their performance but in His own unchangeable character, as fixed and reliable as the sun's rising and the stars' courses.
basic.crossReferences: Zechariah 3:8 and 6:12 take up the same title, the Branch, for the same coming Davidic figure. 1 Corinthians 1:30 declares that Christ Himself has become our righteousness, wisdom, sanctification, and redemption. Luke 1:32-33 promises Jesus an eternal throne on David's line, fulfilling the everlasting dynasty promised here. Hebrews 7:23-25 shows Christ's own priesthood permanently securing what the Levitical priesthood, promised here to continue, could only foreshadow.
basic.oneStory: From a prison cell in a city about to fall, God promises not only that Jerusalem will be rebuilt but that a righteous Branch of David will come, under whom His people will be known by His own righteousness. That promise is fulfilled in Christ, the eternal Son of David who is Himself our righteousness and whose priesthood secures forever what Levi's could only foreshadow.

context.literary: A second word to Jeremiah while still imprisoned, structured as three linked oracles: healing for the city, the Righteous Branch and Davidic-Levitical covenant, and a final assurance answering the people's own despairing words that God had rejected them.
context.historical: Delivered during the same siege as chapter 32, when Jerusalem's fall was imminent and the Davidic line itself appeared about to be extinguished, making the promise of an everlasting throne and priesthood a direct answer to the crisis at hand.
context.canonical: This chapter's righteous Branch echoes and expands Jeremiah 23:5-6, and its title, The LORD Our Righteousness, is later taken up by Zechariah's picture of the Branch, 3:8, 6:12, forming a thread that runs directly into New Testament messianic expectation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reassure a people facing the apparent end of their nation, city, and royal line that God's covenant promises to David and to Israel remain unbroken, secured by His own character rather than by anything Judah has done or could do.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is prophetic promise concerning specific historical and messianic realities, the rebuilding of Jerusalem and the coming of the Davidic Branch; it does not prescribe a ritual or behavior for believers to perform. It does establish the enduring truth that God's covenant faithfulness cannot be broken by human failure.

debates.question: Does the promise that David will never lack a man to sit on the throne, verse 17, require an unbroken line of literal Davidic kings on an earthly throne?

views.label: Fulfilled in Christ's eternal reign, not a continuous earthly dynasty
views.case: The Davidic throne was in fact vacant for centuries after the exile; the New Testament resolves this by identifying Jesus, the final Son of David, as the one who reigns forever, Luke 1:32-33, so the promise is kept in Him rather than through an unbroken succession of literal kings.

views.label: A promise whose ultimate guarantee is a still-future, visible Davidic reign
views.case: Some hold the promise anticipates a future, earthly reign of the Davidic Messiah still to come, in addition to His present spiritual reign, since the language of verse 16 speaks of Jerusalem dwelling securely in terms not yet fully realized.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the promise is secured in Christ, the true Son of David; they differ chiefly on whether its fullest, visible realization is entirely present now or still partly future, a question that does not diminish the certainty of the promise itself.

study.originalLanguages: God promises a tsemach, a Branch, that will sprout for David when the dynasty seems as good as cut down, and gives the city itself the name yahwehTsidqenu, the LORD Our Righteousness, tying its security entirely to a righteousness not its own. The whole promise is anchored in the beritYom, God's covenant with the day and the night, as fixed and unbreakable as creation's own order.

typology.type: The righteous Branch of David, Jeremiah 33:15
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the Son of David who administers true justice and righteousness, taken up again in Zechariah 3:8 and 6:12 before the New Testament identifies Jesus as this Branch.

typology.type: The city named The LORD Our Righteousness, Jeremiah 33:16
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, who Paul says has become our righteousness before God, 1 Corinthians 1:30, so that believers, not the city alone, now bear this name as their standing before God.

study.christ: The righteous Branch of David promised here, who will administer justice and righteousness and give Jerusalem the very name The LORD Our Righteousness, is fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the Son of David whose throne is established forever, Luke 1:32-33. Paul draws directly on this promise's logic when he writes that Christ has become for us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption, 1 Corinthians 1:30, so that what Jerusalem was promised as a city, believers now possess in the person of Christ Himself.
study.god: God hides His face in righteous anger over Judah's sin, yet in the same breath promises health, healing, full forgiveness, and a righteous king to come. He grounds His covenant with David and the Levites not in Israel's faithfulness, which this book has shown to be broken again and again, but in His own unchangeable character, as fixed as the daily order of the sun and the stars.

crossReferences.note: Takes up the same title, the Branch, for the same coming Davidic figure, extending Jeremiah's promise into a later generation's hope.
Names the Branch again as the one who will build the temple of the LORD and rule as both king and priest.
Declares that Christ Himself has become our righteousness, the fulfillment of the name given to the city in this chapter.
Promises Jesus an eternal throne on David's line, fulfilling the everlasting dynasty promised in Jeremiah 33:17.

study.oneStory: From a prison cell in a city under siege, God promises not merely rebuilding but a righteous Branch of David, a king under whom His people will be known by the LORD's own righteousness rather than their own. That promise reaches its fulfillment in Christ, the eternal Son of David and our righteousness, whose priesthood secures forever what Levi's could only foreshadow and point toward.

quiz.q: What invitation does God give Jeremiah at the start of this chapter, verses 2 to 3?

options.text: To call on Him, and He will answer and show great and unsearchable things Jeremiah does not know.
options.note: Verse 3 gives this specific invitation, grounded in God's identity as the LORD who made the earth.

options.text: To stop praying entirely since the city's fate is already sealed.
options.note: The invitation is the opposite, an active call to pray and seek unsearchable things from God.

options.text: To flee the city immediately before the siege ends.
options.note: Jeremiah remains imprisoned throughout; the invitation concerns prayer, not escape.

options.text: To perform a specific ritual sacrifice for the city's protection.
options.note: The invitation is to call on God in prayer, not to perform a ritual sacrifice.

quiz.q: What does God promise for the ruined city of Jerusalem in verses 6 to 13?

options.text: Health, healing, forgiveness of sin, and the return of joy, celebration, and ordinary life to its deserted streets.
options.note: Verses 6 to 11 describe this full restoration in vivid, concrete terms of joy and renewed daily life.

options.text: That the city would remain a permanent, uninhabited ruin.
options.note: The promise is the opposite, restoration, healing, and renewed habitation.

options.text: That only the palace, not the ordinary city, would be rebuilt.
options.note: The promise includes the whole city and surrounding countryside, not the palace alone.

options.text: That the healing would come without any forgiveness of sin.
options.note: Verse 8 explicitly includes cleansing from iniquity and forgiveness of sin as part of the promised restoration.

quiz.q: What does the righteous Branch of verses 15 to 16 do, and what name is given to Jerusalem under Him?

options.text: He administers justice and righteousness in the land, and Jerusalem is called The LORD Our Righteousness.
options.note: Verses 15 to 16 give both His work and the specific new name for the secured city.

options.text: He abolishes the Davidic line entirely to establish a new dynasty.
options.note: The Branch is explicitly said to sprout for David, continuing rather than abolishing the Davidic line.

options.text: He is given the name The LORD Our Righteousness Himself in this specific chapter.
options.note: In this chapter it is the city, Jerusalem, that receives this name; Jeremiah 23:6 gives the same name to the Branch directly.

options.text: He rules only over Israel's enemies, not over Judah itself.
options.note: The Branch's rule of justice and righteousness is specifically over the land of Judah and Jerusalem, not merely over enemy nations.

quiz.q: What guarantees the certainty of God's covenant with David and the Levites in verses 19 to 22?

options.text: It is compared to God's fixed covenant with the day and the night, unbreakable as long as day and night keep their appointed times.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 explicitly ground the Davidic and Levitical covenant in this fixed, unbreakable natural order.

options.text: It depends entirely on the continued good behavior of David's descendants.
options.note: The guarantee is grounded in God's own fixed order of creation, not in the descendants' behavior.

options.text: It is left conditional and uncertain throughout this section.
options.note: The passage presents the covenant as certain, using the impossibility of breaking day and night as its very proof.

options.text: It rests on a majority vote among the tribes of Israel.
options.note: The guarantee comes from God's own unbreakable created order, not from any human vote or decision.

quiz.q: How does this chapter answer the people's claim that God has rejected the two families He had chosen, verses 24 to 26?

options.text: God says rejecting Israel's descendants is as impossible as breaking His covenant with the fixed order of heaven and earth, so He will restore them and show compassion.
options.note: Verses 25 to 26 directly answer the despairing claim with this specific, grounded assurance.

options.text: God confirms that He has indeed permanently rejected them.
options.note: God explicitly denies this claim, grounding His denial in the fixed order of creation itself.

options.text: God remains silent and gives no response to this claim.
options.note: Verses 25 to 26 give a direct, explicit response to the people's despairing claim.

options.text: God says the rejection applies only to the house of Israel, not the house of Judah.
options.note: The response addresses both the descendants of Jacob and of David together, not one house alone.

questions: God invited Jeremiah to call on Him and be shown great and unsearchable things, even from a prison cell. What would it look like for you to call on God expectantly in your own confined or difficult circumstances?
God promised full healing and forgiveness for a city under deserved judgment. Where do you need to receive God's forgiveness rather than continuing to carry guilt He has already promised to remove?
The city was to be renamed The LORD Our Righteousness, a righteousness not its own. Where are you relying on your own righteousness instead of resting in the righteousness Christ gives?

God grounded His unbreakable covenant in the fixed order of day and night. What circumstance in your life makes God's promises feel uncertain, and how does His own unchanging character speak to that uncertainty?
The people despaired, saying God had rejected them completely. Where have you spoken despair over your own situation that God's word actually contradicts?

translationNotes.note: Great and unsearchable things renders a Hebrew phrase suggesting fortified, inaccessible things, secrets normally hidden, which God offers to reveal to those who call on Him even in dire circumstances.

The name The LORD Our Righteousness given here to the city matches, word for word, the name given to the Branch Himself in Jeremiah 23:6, linking the security of the city inseparably to the righteousness of its coming king.

The appeal to an unbreakable covenant with the day and the night uses the most stable, observable phenomenon available to ancient hearers as proof of God's own unbreakable faithfulness to His promises.
